When people imagine the modern school shooter, they usually think of the postc coline archetype. Perpetrators who are isolated and misanthropic while obsessed with notoriety. Eric Harris and Dan Clelebold became the reference point for how many later cases were understood. But one of the most disturbing school shooting cases in American history happened before Coline ever took place. In 1998 in Springfield, Oregon, 15-year-old Kiplan Kingl murdered his parents before opening fire at Thirsten High School. Unlike later attackers who seemed to chase infamy or imitate previous killers, King's case emerged from a collapsing home life, untreated psychosis, and a teenage mind spiraling into paranoia. To understand how a seemingly ordinary community became the site of one of the late 1990s most haunting tragedies, I've compiled a full research iceberg that follows the case from the attack itself to the lesserknown details surrounding it, even with potential insight as to what caused the massive attack that preceded it. This is a Kipkinko iceberg. 215 1998. On May 21st, 1998, 15-year-old Kipland Kip King walked into the cafeteria of Thirstston High School in Springfield, Oregon, armed with a semi-automatic rifle. He opened fire on his classmates, killing two students, Ben Walker and Macau Nickson, and wounding 25 others. By the time police began piecing together what had happened, they would discover the attackers started the day before when King murdered both of his parents at the family home. his parents. The thirst and shooting did not begin at the school. It began inside the Kinkle family home. After Kif was suspended for bringing a stolen gun to school, he returned home and shot his father, Bill. Later that evening, when his mother, Faith, came home, he killed her, too. Their bodies remained in the house overnight while King prepared for what he was going to do the next morning. Suspension/expulsion. The immediate trigger for the thirst and shooting came the day before the attack when King brought a stolen handgun to school. He had paid another student $110 for the weapon, but the gun was discovered and Kinko was suspended. That suspension set off the final collapse. After being sent home, he took the lives of both his parents, then returned to Thirsten High School the next morning armed for the attack. Jacob Reicher. As King's rifle ran empty and he began to reload, wounded student Jacob Reiker tackled him. His brother Dustin Riker along with Adam Wahberger, Doug Yor, and other students rushed to help pin Kinkle to the cafeteria floor. During the struggle, Kingle pulled a Glock from his belt and fired one shot before being disarmed, wounding Riker again and injuring another student. As the students restrained him, Kingle shouted, "Just kill me." They held him down until police arrived and arrested him. His sister, Kip King's older sister, Kristen Kinkl, was six years older than him and was studying at Hawaii Pacific University at the time of the Thirsten High School shooting. After the attack, she wrote a letter to Judge Jack Madison, giving one of the most personal accounts of Kip's life before the murders. In the letter, Kristen described Kip as compassionate, sensitive, and eager to please the people around him. She said that only with hindsight could she recognize the signs that he had needed a kind of help his family did not know how to give. Her letter does not deny the horror of what he did, but it presents him as someone whose mental collapse had been hidden beneath the surface of what seemed like a normal family life. She also described visiting him after the attack, saying that for weeks he could barely make eye contact. When he finally spoke, he told her, "I am so sorry." According to Kristen, Kip wanted to listen to the victim's families during the hearings because he believed he owed it to them. This entry is important because Kristen's letter gives the case a rare perspective from inside the family. She was not only grieving the murder of her parents, she was also trying to understand how the little brother she remembered had become capable of killing them. His sentence Kipkle was sentenced to 111 years and 8 months of the possibility of parole. The number came from the structure of the sentence. 25 years for the four murder convictions which would be served at the same time followed by consecutive sentences for the 26 attempted murder counts. In practice, this meant King would spend the rest of his life in prison with his earliest release date placed more than a century after the shooting, January 21st, 2110. Paranoid schizophrenia diagnosis. After the attack, Kingle was looked at by multiple mental health experts. Court documents state that the experts genuinely agreed he suffered from psychosis and some form of paranoid schizophrenia or a schizopeeffective disorder combining features of schizophrenia and depression. King reported that he had been hearing voices since he was around 12 years old, including one voice that encouraged violence. According to court records, he said the voice had instructed him to commit the murders and the school shooting and that he felt he had no choice but to obey it. Following the recommendation of a child psychologist, Kinko began taking antiscychotic medicine. In July 1999, his attorneys instructed him to stop taking the medication so experts could assess his mental condition more accurately. Court records say his hallucinations and depression worsened after the withdrawal, partly because of the stress of the approaching trial. This diagnosis became central to the legal and moral debate around the case. King eventually entered a plea agreement in which the aggravated murder and attempted aggravated murder charges were dismissed while he pleaded guilty to murder and attempted murder. The result was a sentence that treated him as legally responsible while still leaving the question of his mental illness at the center of the case. Confession. One of the most significant pieces of media connected to the case is King's recorded confession. It was taped by detective Al Waran on the day of the Thirstston High School shooting. Shortly after, Kingle had been taken into custody. The confession gives limited factual detail about the attack, but it offers a disturbing look at Kingle's mental state in the immediate aftermath. Throughout the recording, he repeatedly expresses suicidal thoughts and appears overwhelmed by what he has done. >> Like, what what was he saying about you? >> [clears throat] [laughter] >> The tape captures the true collapse of his mind. The recording ends abruptly when Kingo asks for water, leaving behind one of the most unsettling documents in the case, the 111/12year sentence confusion. A smaller point of confusion around Kinko's sentence is a difference between 111 years and 112 years. The exact sentence was 111 years and 8 months, but many sources round that figure up and describe it as a 112 years sentence. This is why both numbers appear in discussions of the case, even though they refer to the same prison term. Varying psychological opinions. A major part of Kinko's later legal debate came from the different psychological opinions given about his condition. Experts generally agreed that he had serious mental health problems, but they differed on what that meant for his future. Pediatric neurologist Richard Conor testified that Kinkle showed abnormalities in language and sensory processing. He believed these problems suggested damage in parts of the brain linked to overall impulse control, making Kingle more vulnerable to psychotic episodes. Conor also said that because King was still a juvenile, his prognosis could be hopeful of proper treatment and medication. Other experts were more cautious. Child psychologist Orin Bolstad said Kinko's illness was treatable but not fully curable and that his hallucinations improved with antiscychotic medication. However, he also said he would not want to see Kinkle released without medication and intensive support. Psychiatrist William Sack gave a similar view saying Kingle could potentially be managed after decades of treatment but would remain dangerous if his illness was not properly controlled. This shows why the Kingle case became so legally complicated. It was not just a question of whether he was mentally ill, but whether a teenage offender with severe psychosis could ever be safely rehabilitated after committing such extreme violence. Fixation on guns and explosives. King's obsession with guns and explosives appeared long before the thirst and shooting. It was discovered that he listed surfing the web for info on how to create explosives as his occupation on an internet service account he'd created. In middle school, classmates even voted him most likely to start World War II, a joke that did not age well in the slightest. Several students later described how often Kingle talked about weapons. Jesse Cannon, a 15-year-old thirsten student who rode the bus with him, said King once spoke about bringing guns to school, taking a few lives, and then saving one bullet for himself. Cannon said that the conversation felt so unreal at the time, like they were playing a video game. Other classmates recalled seeing Kingle with guns outside school, shooting at birds and animals along rural roads. 13-year-old TJ Hardy claimed King once showed him a pipe bomb and said he was going to blow something up. Others remembered him bragging about killing animals or describing hunting in unusually graphic terms. After the attack, police searching the King home discovered more explosive paraphernalia. Officers found explosive devices inside the house and called in bomb disposable experts. One device was disarmed while another was reportedly so large that part of the house had to be cut away so it could be removed safely. Investigators also found booby traps, bomb making materials, related literature, a hand grenade, and a case of Howard's shell canisters. The discovery showed that the violence at thirst in high school was not the only danger King had created. Behind the school shooting was a much broader fixation on weapons and destruction. This entry matters because King's fixation on violence was not invisible to the people around him. It often came disguised as jokes or just general teen edgginess. Only after the attack did these jokes begin to look like warnings. Walk through. Another significant piece of media from the case is the police walkthrough filmed on the day of the attack. Detective Al Wan took Kingle back through Thirstston High School and had him retrace his movements. During the walkthrough, Kinko explained the attack as investigators led him through the school. The transcript shows King giving mostly short, flat answers. He does not offer a detailed explanation for the attack. When asked why he did it, his response is essentially that he felt he had no other choice. >> Why did you do this? >> Had no other choice. >> He also denied that any specific student had upset him or that anyone had threatened him into carrying out the shooting. What makes this walkthrough unsettling is how clinical it is. Investigators move through the aftermath of the tragedy while King quietly confirms parts of what happened. He was acting extremely childlike, as if he was being corrected for ordinary misbehavior rather than someone who had just committed a massacre. Early learning difficulties and IQ. Kingo's early life was shaped by a sharp contrast between family expectations and his own learning difficulties. Both of his parents were respected local teachers and people close to the family later described the King household as one where achievement was quietly expected. Kip reportedly told a psychiatrist he felt he had to be 100% and that failing to do his best made him feel like an embarrassment to his parents. From early childhood, Kinkle struggled in ways that did not fit the image of a successful teacher's son. He had difficulty learning to tie his shoes, struggled badly with reading, writing, and spelling, and repeated first grade because of concerns about maturity and development. His sister Kristen later remembered him studying for spelling tests for hours and still failing, leaving him confused and ashamed because he did not understand what was wrong. By third grade, Kinko was diagnosed with a learning disorder and his parents also suspected attention deficit issues. In middle school, he told friends he was taking ritland to control his temper. In the fourth grade, Kip continued to qualify for special education services and was diagnosed with a learning disability. He worked with a special education counselor for the year. However, according to his fourth grade teacher, he was simultaneously placed in a talented and gifted program because of his above average performance in science and maths. At the same time, later psychological testimony made clear that his problems were not a simple lack of intelligence. Psychiatrist William Sack described him as cognitively bright and said his overall IQ was high despite his learning disability. This is an interesting cognitive profile which complicates the image of Kingle adding nuance to the typical simply disturbed or simply unintelligent labels. Kinkle versus person. Kinkle versus person was one of the major legal battles over Kip King Kinkle's sentence after the Supreme Court restricted life without parole sentences for juveniles in cases like Graham versus Florida and Miller versus Alabama. King argued that his 111year and 8-month sentence functioned as a life sentence for a crime committed when he was 15. His argument centered on whether the law should give him a meaningful chance at release given his age and severe mental illness. In 2018, the Oregon Supreme Court rejected that argument. The court ruled that his sentence was not unconstitutional because the original sentencing judge had considered his youth and psychological condition before imposing it. The court also focused on the scale of the violence. Kinko had been sentenced for four murders and 26 attempted murders, which made his case different from the juvenile sentencing cases that involved a single killing or non-homicide crimes. The most controversial part of the ruling was his treatment of his mental illness. The court accepted that Kingle had a serious disorder, but emphasized that it was treatable rather than curable and that he could remain dangerous without medication and intensive structure. In effect, Kingle versus person became a debate over whether a severely mentally ill juvenile offender should ever receive a realistic path to release after committing extreme violence. Shoplifting. Along with some friends, Kip got caught shoplifting CDs from the local Target store. Not much else is known about this incident. Keeping houses. Two weeks before the Thirstston High School shooting, Kinko and several friends toilet papered a house in Springfield, Oregon. According to later accounts, they used 400 rolls of toilet paper trying to beat a local school record. The prank seemed minor at first, but it became important because of what followed. After Kingo got in trouble, his parents grounded him. Around this period, his father discovered a locked box containing weapons, including items Kinko had been hiding from his family. This entry matters because it shows how close the warning signs were to the surface. What began as a teenage prank led back to King's hidden weapons only days before the violence at Thirsten High School. Brain abnormalities. A deeper part of Kinkle's psychological profile came from testimony about possible brain abnormalities. Neurologist Richard Conor examined Kinkle and said he found several signs of neurological dysfunction. These included problems with language, spelling, attention, motor control, sensory processing, and reflexes. Concord also noted subtle physical asymmetry with the left side of Kinko's body appearing slightly smaller than the right. In his view, this suggested the possibility of early developmental problems in the brain rather than a recent injury. Because the left side of the body is controlled by the right side of the brain, some of the findings pointed towards dysfunction in the right hemisphere, a spec scan was also performed. Concil said that the scan showed decreased activity in several areas, especially the preffrontal, temporal, and partial regions. He described the frontal loes as central to executive function, planning, inhibition, decision-m, and the ability to control behavior over time. He also connected the temporal loes to memory, emotion, language, and sensory distortion. Congo's overall conclusion was that King had multiple areas of impaired neurological function, especially in regions connected to inhibition, emotional control, and high level thinking. He believed these deficits could make Kingle more vulnerable to psychotic episodes, while still saying there was hope for improvement with medication, structure, and proper treatment. Romeo and Juliet. King's connection to Romeo and Juliet appeared in several parts of the case. Before leaving for Thirsten High School on the morning of the shooting, he turned the song Leaptood, translated Love Death, from the 1996 film. The play also appeared in his schoolwork. In one assignment about love at first sight, King wrote that love at first sight existed only in movies where the people in the movies are better than you. In the same essay, he described love as something that does more harm than good, adding that love was a horrible thing because it could make people kill and hate. King also wrote from the perspective of Tibol, Juliet's violent cousin. In that assignment, he gave Tibol lines about hatred, anger, and the feeling of killing another person. The most disturbing line comes after Tibultt takes a life when King has him say that he loved it because it released his anger. This entry is not important because Romeo and Juda caused anything. It matters because Kinkle seemed drawn to the parts of the story where love, violence, murder, and suicide collapsed into each other. In his writing, the play became another place where his loneliness and violent fantasies surfaced before the attack. Prozac. Before the shooting, King's parents tried to get him help. They enrolled him in anger management and had him evaluated by psychologist Jeffrey Hicks. Over several months, Kinkle was prescribed Prozac. At first, the treatment appeared to help. After around eight weeks of sessions, Kinkle, his mother, and Hicks agreed that he was doing well enough to stop regular therapy. But when his Prozac prescription ran out in late 1997, Kingle did not refill it. After the attack, his medication history became one of the more debated parts of the case. Some critics pointed to Prozac or to his decision to stop taking it as a possible factor in his decline. But reducing the case to medication alone misses the larger picture. King was dealing with severe psychological distress and a vast array of issues. This entry matters because Prozac became part of the argument over what warning signs were missed. It was not a simple explanation for what happened, but it was one piece of a much wider collapse. Rocks over an overpass. King's anger management treatment began after an earlier criminal incident in Bend, Oregon. While attending a snowboarding clinic with a friend, Kinkle was accused of throwing rocks from a highway overpass. One of the rocks struck a car passing below. According to accounts of the arrest, police found Kinkle's friend near the overpass and later found Kinkle back at the motel where the boys were staying. When confronted, Kinko reportedly began crying and immediately asked whether anyone had been hurt. He claimed that his friend had thrown the rock that hit the car, but both boys were charged. This incident became important because it was one of Kinko's first serious contacts with the justice system. Afterward, his parents enrolled him in the anger management that we previously mentioned. Neighbors testimonies. Counts from Kinkle's neighbors show how contradictory his public image was before the shooting. Some remembered him as quiet and harmless. Nancy Carvin, whose daughter had played with him for years, described him as a little skinny kid who had always seemed nice. She said he helped with people's yards and played hide-and-seek around the neighborhood. Other neighbors remembered more disturbing behavior. Betty Anderson said Kingle had once come to her son's 15th birthday party, given him a tool used to break into cars and sprayed the word kill on the driveway with whipped cream. After that, she said he was the one child she told her son not to bring back to the house. This entry matters because it shows how uneven the warning signs looked from the outside. To some people, Kinkle still appeared like an awkward local kid. To others, his behavior had already crossed a line. Kinkle versus Lawhead. Kinkle versus Lawhead was one of Kip Kinkle's postconviction challenges after his original sentence. Unlike Kinkle versus Person, which focused mainly on juvenile sentencing, Lawhead focused on whether his guilty plea should have been accepted in the first place. Kinkle argued that his lawyers had failed him during the plea process. His attorneys had instructed him to stop taking antiscychotic medication so experts could evaluate his mental condition more accurately. Court records later noted that his hallucinations and depression worsened during this period, partly because of the medication withdrawal and the stress of the approaching trial. Because of this, Kinkle claimed that he had not been competent to plead guilty and that his plea was not truly knowing or voluntary. He also argued that the court should not have accepted the plea without proper consent from his guardian, Adllet, who had been appointed because King was still a juvenile. The court rejected the challenge. They found that despite his mental illness, there was not enough evidence that Kinko had been unable to understand the plea process or assist his lawyers, his attorneys, the trial court, and the mental health experts involved at the time had not treated him as legally incompetent Jonesboro shootings. Just 2 months before thirst, another school shooting occurred on March 24th, 1998. 13-year-old Michelle Johnson and 11-year-old Andrew Golden opened fire at Westside Middle School near Jonesboro, Arkansas, taking the lives of five people and wounding 10 others. According to one of King's friends, Kip watched coverage of the Jonesboro shooting and reacted with disturbing interest. The friend claimed that Kinkle looked at the news and said something like, "Hey, that's pretty cool." Journal. Writings from Kinkle's journal share insight on his mindset before and after killing his parents before the murders. His writing is dominated by loneliness and his growing self-hatred. In one entry, he writes, "I sit here all alone. I am always alone." The repetition makes the feeling seem inescapable, as if isolation has become the center of his entire identity. He also frames his suffering in almost religious terms, crying out to God as though his problems have moved beyond ordinary human help. He writes, "Oh God, I am so close to killing people." before asking why God wanted him to live in complete misery. The entry then turns back to weapons. After mentioning that his parents were trying to take away some of his guns, Kinko writes that his guns are the only thing that haven't stabbed me in the back. That line is one of the most revealing in the journal. It shows how much his thinking had warped. People had become sources of rejection and betrayal while weapons have become the only things he felt he could trust. He writes that his eyes hurt and feel like they're trying to crawl out of his head. And at this point, the entry reads like a mind collapsing under paranoia and despair. After killing his parents, Kingle's writing shifts from resentment into panic and guilt. He opens by admitting what he has done, then immediately collapses into confusion. Quote, "I have just killed my parents. I don't know what is happening." The entry is disturbing because he does not write about them with hatred. He says he loves them, calling them wonderful people and describes himself as a horrible son who did not deserve them. His explanation is fractured. Part of him seems to believe he killed them because the shame of his arrest would have destroyed them. He writes that his parents could not handle the embarrassment of his two felony charges, but the rest of the entry points to something much deeper than shame. He says that his head just doesn't work right. Curses the voice inside his head and repeats that he wants his life to end. The most chilling contradiction comes when he writes, "I want to be gone, but I have to kill people. I don't know why." That line captures the collapse of King ruined by guilt and violent compulsion all existing at once. He apologizes repeatedly but still ends with the same fatal logic that he mentioned in the walkthrough. I have no other choice. This journal entry is important because it shows King immediately after the first murders before the school shooting had even happened. He's not presenting a clear ideology or a coherent motive. Unlike most other mass shooters who write something before they commit their attack, he's presenting his mind in crisis. Aware that he has destroyed his family but still convinced that more violence is somehow inevitable. How to join Church of Satan. One obscure detail from King's school life involves a speech class assignment. Kinko reportedly gave a how-to speech about making a bomb, which should have been an obvious warning sign. But according to students in the class, the presentation did not stand out as much as it should have, partly because another student had given a speech on how to join the Church of Satan Disneyland. One of the stranger details from King's psychological evaluations was his fixation on Disney. Dr. Orin Bolstad said that when he asked Kinko about the Disney corporation, Kinkle became unusually animated. Up to that point, his effect had been mostly flat, but the subject of Disney seemed to trigger a much more intense response. Kinko claimed that the Disney dollar would eventually take over the American dollar, and that Disney was gaining control over news and entertainment. He connected the company to censorship, corporate power, and government control while describing Mickey Mouse as a harmless mascot that allowed Disney to avoid criticism. when he repeated the phrase Disneyland, the happiest place on earth. Bolstad said he did it in a cynical and agitated way. The Disney fixation also blended into wider paranoid ideas. Kinkle talked about censorship, dictators, government experiments, and microchips being placed in people. He even said that when he had an MRI, he wondered whether doctors would find a chip in his head. From there, he connected the idea of a chip to the voices he was hearing, suggesting that maybe this was how he was being controlled. Letters from prison. In 2017, Kinkle replied to a 16-year-old girl who had written to him while struggling with her own mental health. The letter is significant because of his tone. Kinkle does not encourage her fixation on him or romanticize what happened. Instead, he tells her to ask for an adult for help, especially if she's hearing voices or feeling trapped inside her own thoughts. He also writes that he would normally not respond to a 16-year-old, but that her letter worried him enough to make an exception. He urges her to take what he says seriously, to trust someone close to her, and to hold on because things can get better. This entry matters because it shows King decades after the attack, looking back on his own childhood through the lens of regret. The letter does not undo what he did, but it shows how his case continued to intersect with questions of mental illness and the possibility of someone warning another person away from the same path. His true motive, one of the clearest motives running through the Kingill case was suicide. He did not appear to imagine the attack ending with escape or survival. During the shooting, after students tackled and restrained him, Kingle shouted for them to kill him. The same pattern continued after his arrest. During questioning, Kingle lunged the detective Al Wan with a knife he had hidden on his body. This has often been read as another suic act, not an attempt to escape, but an attempt to force police into killing him. His journal points in the same direction. After murdering his parents, Kinkle repeatedly wrote that he wanted to die and wanted to be gone while also claiming he still to kill people. That contradiction is central to the attack. It was not driven by one clean motive, but suicide with one of his strongest forces, Alvaro Castillo. One connection sometimes noted between Kipkle and Alvaro Castillo is the day of Castello's attack. On August 30th, 2006, 18-year-old Alvaro Raphael Castillo killed his father at home before opening fire at Orange High School in North Carolina. August 30th is also Kip King's birthday. Castillo was known to be heavily fixated on Coline, but there is no clear evidence that the date was chosen because of King. For that reason, it should not be presented as part of a direct copycat chain. At most, it remains an eerie coincidence. The Becoming by 9in Nails. One small detail connected to Kinko's writings is a possible link to 9-in nails song the becoming from the downward spiral. After the case, some noticed that one of Kinko's lines about the voices in his head sounded similar to a lyric from the song this voice inside my head. This does not mean the music caused the attack. King may have latched on to music that reflected feelings he already had. Depression, alienation, self-hatred, and a fractured sense of identity. The downward spiral is filled with imagery of psychological collapse. So it is not surprising that someone in kinkle state might have found parts of it relatable. This matters because it shows how easily cultural material can become part of the debate after a tragedy. Some people look for blame in media, but the more useful question is why certain lyrics resonated with him in the first place. In King's case, the connection seems less like a cause and more like a reflection of the darkness already present in his own writing. Possible cause of Coline. One of the more speculative entries in this case is Kip's possible connection to the imagery that later became associated with Coline. Kingo wore a trench coat during the Thirstston attack because it allowed him to conceal the weapons he was carrying. Less than a year later, the Coline perpetrators would become heavily associated with trench coats. Even though there is no solid evidence that thisa specific detail came from King, there were other overlaps, too. Kingle had an interest in explosives, used bomb-making materials such as the anarchist cookbook, and reportedly listened to Marilyn Manson. All of which became later part of the cultural discussion around Coline. But these similarities need to be handled carefully. They may reflect the same late 1990s atmosphere, a violent media panic, alienated teenage identity, and fascination with mass destruction rather than direct influence. This becomes interesting when placed beside Eric Harris's own insistence that Coline was not meant to be a copycat attack. In his writings, Harris directly said that they were not trying to copy anyone. But that claim was never entirely convincing because Coline itself was built from a wider culture of influence. The Oklahoma City bombing, violent media, doom, military imagery, and more. The point is not that Kip caused it, but that his case came first and already contained several elements that would later become familiar in the post Coline profile. Hidden weapons, trench co imagery, explosives, violent writings, and a desire to create catastrophic harm. After Coline happened, King was worried that he may have contributed to it. Whether that fear was justified is impossible to prove, but it shows how thirsten sound easily at the edge for changing era. Pekka Eric Alvin connection. One of the most obscure later connections to the Kinko case comes from the Joker school shooting in Finland. After Pekka Eric Alvin carried out the attack in 2007, one of his online friends was left questioning whether she could have done anything to stop him. In a documentary, she explains that she thought of Kipkinkle because he had also attacked his school. But unlike many perpetrators, he had survived and was still in prison. She decided to write to him hoping he might understand whether anyone could have recognized the warning signs or prevented what happened. King apparently wrote back. His answer was blunt. There was nothing his own friends could have done and he believed there was nothing she could have done to stop Pekky either. This entry is important because it turns Kinko into strange point of reference long after Thirsten. His case was not just remembered as a pre- Cololine shooting. It became something later people connected to similar tragedies and looked back to when trying to understand guilt warning signs and whether intervention had ever truly been possible. And with that, the iceberg is finished. Thank you for watching until the very end. I really appreciate it. Make sure to subscribe and follow my Twitter if you enjoyed, and I'll see you in the next video.
